greek_head: Ἔχεις μοι εἰπεῖν ὦ Σώκρατες ἆρα διδακτὸν ἡ ἀρετή ἢ οὐ διδακτὸν ἀλλ' ἀσκητόν ἢ οὔτε ἀσκητὸν οὔτε
greek_tokens: exis egw eipon eimi swkraths ara didaktos o areth h ou didaktos alla askhtos h oute askhtos oute maqhtos alla fusis paragignomai o anqrwpos h allos tis tropos eimi menw pro o men qessalos eudokimos eimi en o ellhn kai qaumazw epi ippikos te kai ploutos
english_head: Can you tell me, Socrates, whether virtue can be taught, or is acquired by practice, not teaching? Or if neither by practice nor by learning, whether it comes to mankind by nature or in some other way? Meno, of old the Thessalians were famous and admired among the Greeks for their riding and

greek_head: νῦν δέ ὡς ἐμοὶ δοκεῖ καὶ ἐπὶ σοφίᾳ καὶ οὐχ ἥκιστα οἱ τοῦ σοῦ ἑταίρου Ἀριστίππου πολῖται Λαρισαῖοι τούτου δὲ ὑμῖν
greek_tokens: nun de ws egw dokew kai epi sofia kai ou hkistos e o sew etairos aristippos poliths larisaios outos de su aitios eimi gorgihs afikneomai gar eis o polis erasths epi sofia lambanw aleuadwn te o proteros os o sos erasths eimi aristippos kai o allos qessalos kai dh kai outos o eqos su eqizw afobos te kai megaloprephs apokrinw ean tis tis eromai wsper eoika o
english_head: their riches; but now they have a name, I believe, for wisdom also, especially your friend Aristippus’s people, the Larisaeans. For this you have to thank Gorgias: for when he came to that city he made the leading men of the Aleuadae—among them your lover Aristippus—and the Thessalians generally enamored of wisdom. Nay more, he has given you the regular habit of answering any chance question in a fearless, magnificent manner, as befits those who know:

greek_head: εἰδότας ἅτε καὶ αὐτὸς παρέχων αὑτὸν ἐρωτᾶν τῶν Ἑλλήνων τῷ βουλομένῳ ὅτι ἄν τις βούληται καὶ οὐδενὶ ὅτῳ οὐκ
greek_tokens: oida ate kai autos paraxwnnumi eautou erwtaw o ellhn o boulomai ostis an tis boulomai kai oudeis ostis ou apokrinw enqade de eimi filos menw o enantion periisthmi wsper auxmos tis o sofia gignomai kai kinduneuw
english_head: for he sets the example of offering himself to be questioned by any Greek who chooses, and on any point one likes, and he has an answer for everybody. Now in this place, my dear Meno, we have a contrary state of things:

greek_head: ἐκ τῶνδε τῶν τόπων παρ' ὑμᾶς οἴχεσθαι ἡ σοφία εἰ γοῦν τινα ἐθέλεις οὕτως ἐρέσθαι τῶν ἐνθάδε οὐδεὶς ὅστις οὐ
greek_tokens: ek ode o topos para su oixomai o sofia ei goun tis eqelw outws eromai o enqade oudeis ostis ou gelaw kai eraomai eimi cenos kinduneuw su dokew makarios tis eimi areth goun eite didaktos eiqe ostis tropos paragignomai oida egw de tosoutos dew eite didaktos eite mh didaktos oida wste oude autos ostis pote eimi o parapan areth tugxanw eidoi
english_head: a drought of wisdom, as it were, has come on; and it seems as though wisdom had deserted our borders in favour of yours. You have only to ask one of our people a question such as that, and he will be sure to laugh and say: Stranger, you must think me a specially favoured mortal, to be able to tell whether virtue can be taught, or in what way it comes to one: so far am I from knowing whether it can be taught or not, that I actually do not even know what the thing itself, virtue, is at all.

greek_head: Ἐγὼ οὖν καὶ αὐτός ὦ Μένων οὕτως ἔχω συμπένομαι τοῖς πολίταις τούτου τοῦ πράγματος καὶ ἐμαυτὸν καταμέμφομαι
greek_tokens: egw oun kai autos eimi menw outws exw sumpenomai o poliths outos o pragma kai emautou katamemfomai ws ou eidoi peri aretaw o parapan o de mh oida tis eimi pws an opoios ge tis oida h dokew su oios te eimi ostis menwn mh gignwskw o parapan ostis eimi outos oida eite kalos eite plousios eite kai gennaios eimi eite kai enantios outos dokew su oios su eimi ou egw alla su eimi swkraths alhqhs
english_head: And I myself, Meno, am in the same case; I share my townsmen’s poverty in this matter: I have to reproach myself with an utter ignorance about virtue; and if I do not know what a thing is, how can I know what its nature may be? Or do you imagine it possible, if one has no cognizance at all of Meno, that one could know whether he is handsome or rich or noble, or the reverse of these? Do you suppose that one could? Not I. But is it true, Socrates,

greek_head: οὐδ' ὅτι ἀρετή ἐστιν οἶσθα ἀλλὰ ταῦτα περὶ σοῦ καὶ οἴκαδε ἀπαγγέλλωμεν
greek_tokens: oude ostis areth eimi oida alla outos peri sew kai oikade apaggellw mh monos ge eimi etairos alla kai ostis oude allos pw entugxanw oida ws egw dokazw tis de gorgihs ou entugxanw oste enqade eimi egw eita ou dokew su oida ou panu eimi mnhmwn eimi menw wste ou exw eipon en o pareimi pws egw tote dokew alla isos ekeinos te oida kai su o ekeinos elegos anamimnhskw oun
english_head: that you do not even know what virtue is? Are we to return home with this report of you? Not only this, my friend, but also that I never yet came across anybody who did know, in my opinion. What? You did not meet Gorgias when he was here? I did. And you didn’t consider that he knew? I have not a very good memory, Meno, so I cannot tell at the moment how he struck me then. It may be that he did know, and that you know what he said:

greek_head: με πῶς ἔλεγεν εἰ δὲ βούλει αὐτὸς εἰπέ δοκεῖ γὰρ δήπου σοὶ ἅπερ ἐκείνῳ
greek_tokens: egw pws legw ei de boulomai autos eipon dokew gar dhpou sos o ekeinos egw ekeinos men toinun eaw epei kai apeimi su de autos eimi pros qea menw tis fhmi areth eimi eipon kai mh fqonew ina eutuxhs yeusma yeudw eimi an fainw su men eidoi kai gorgihs egw de erw mhdeis pwpote oida entugxanw
english_head: remind me therefore how he expressed it; or if you like, make your own statement, for I expect you share his views. I do. Then let us pass him over, since in fact he is not present, and do you tell me, in heaven’s name, what is your own account of virtue. Speak out frankly, that I may find myself the victim of a most fortunate falsehood, if you and Gorgias prove to have knowledge of it, while I have said that I never yet came across anyone who had.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' οὐ χαλεπόν ὦ Σώκρατες εἰπεῖν πρῶτον μέν εἰ βούλει ἀνδρὸς ἀρετήν ῥᾴδιον ὅτι αὕτη ἐστὶν ἀνδρὸς
greek_tokens: alla ou xalepos eimi swkraths eipon proteros men ei boulomai anhr areth radios ostis outos eimi anhr areth ikanos eimi o o polis prassw kai prassw o men filos eu poiew o de exqros kakos kai autos eulabeomai mhdeis toioutos pasxw ei de boulomai gunh areth ou xalepos dierxomai ostis deomai autos o oikia eu oikew swzw te o endon kai kathkoos eimi o anhr kai allos eimi pais areth kai qhlus kai arshn kai presbus anhr ei men boulomai eleuqeros ei de boulomai
english_head: Why, there is no difficulty, Socrates, in telling. First of all, if you take the virtue of a man, it is easily stated that a man’s virtue is this—that he be competent to manage the affairs of his city, and to manage them so as to benefit his friends and harm his enemies, and to take care to avoid suffering harm himself. Or take a woman’s virtue: there is no difficulty in describing it as the duty of ordering the house well, looking after the property indoors, and obeying her husband. And the child has another virtue—one for the female, and one for the male; and there is another for elderly men—one, if you like, for freemen,

greek_head: δούλου καὶ ἄλλαι πάμπολλαι ἀρεταί εἰσιν ὥστε οὐκ ἀπορία εἰπεῖν ἀρετῆς πέρι ὅτι ἐστίν καθ' ἑκάστην γὰρ
greek_tokens: doulos kai allos pampolus areth eimi wste ou aporia eipon aretaw peri ostis eimi kaqa ekastos gar o pracis kai o hlikia pros ekastos ergnumi ekastos egw o areth eimi wsautws de oimh eimi swkraths kai o kakia polus ge tis eutuxia eoika xraw eimi menw ei eis zhtew areth smhnos tis aneuriskw aretaw para sos keimai atar eimi menw kata outos o eikwn o
english_head: and yet another for slaves. And there are very many other virtues besides, so that one cannot be at a loss to explain what virtue is; for it is according to each activity and age that every one of us, in whatever we do, has his virtue; and the same, I take it, Socrates, will hold also of vice. I seem to be in a most lucky way, Meno; for in seeking one virtue I have discovered a whole swarm of virtues there in your keeping. Now, Meno, to follow this figure of a swarm,

greek_head: περὶ τὰ σμήνη εἴ μου ἐρομένου μελίττης περὶ οὐσίας ὅτι ποτ' ἐστίν πολλὰς καὶ παντοδαπὰς ἔλεγες αὐτὰς εἶναι τί
greek_tokens: peri o smhnh ei egw eromai melissa peri ousia ostis pote eimi polus kai pantodapos legw autos eimi tis an apokrinw egw ei su arow ara outos fhmi polus kai pantodapos eimi kai diaferw allhlwn o melissa eimi h outos men oudeis diaferw allos de tis oios h kallos h megeqos h allos tis o toioutos eipon tis an apokrinw outws erwtaw outos egw ostis oudeis diaferw h melissa eimi o eteros o eteros
english_head: suppose I should ask you what is the real nature of the bee, and you replied that there are many different kinds of bees, and I rejoined: Do you say it is by being bees that they are of many and various kinds and differ from each other, or does their difference lie not in that, but in something else—for example, in their beauty or size or some other quality? Tell me, what would be your answer to this question? Why, this—that they do not differ, as bees, the one from the other.

greek_head: Εἰ οὖν εἶπον μετὰ ταῦτα Τοῦτο τοίνυν μοι αὐτὸ εἰπέ ὦ Μένων ᾧ οὐδὲν διαφέρουσιν ἀλλὰ ταὐτόν
greek_tokens: ei oun eipon meta outos outos toinun egw autos eipon eimi menw os oudeis diaferw alla tautos eimi apas tis outos fhmi eimi exw dhpou an tis egw eipon egw outws dh kai peri o aretaw kan ei polus kai pantodapos eimi eis ge tis eidos tautos apas exw dia o eimi areth eis o kalos pou exis apoblepw o apokrinw o erwtaw ekeinos dhlow o tugxanw
english_head: And if I went on to say: Well now, there is this that I want you to tell me, Meno: what do you call the quality by which they do not differ, but are all alike? You could find me an answer, I presume? I could. And likewise also with the virtues, however many and various they may be, they all have one common character whereby they are virtues, and on which one would of course be wise to keep an eye when one is giving a definitive answer to the question of what virtue really is.

greek_head: οὖσα ἀρετή ἢ οὐ μανθάνεις ὅτι λέγω Δοκῶ γέ μοι μανθάνειν οὐ μέντοι ὡς βούλομαί
greek_tokens: eimi areth h ou manqanw ostis legos dokazw ge egw manqanw ou mentoi ws boulomai ge pw kataxwnnumi o erwtaw poteros de peri aretaw monos su outws dokew eimi menw allos men anhr eimi allos de gunh kai o allos h kai peri ugieia kai peri megeqos kai peri isxus wsautws allos men anhr dokew su eimi ugieia allos de gunh h tautos pantaxou eidos eimi ean ugieia
english_head: You take my meaning, do you not? My impression is that I do; but still I do not yet grasp the meaning of the question as I could wish. Is it only in the case of virtue, do you think, Meno, that one can say there is one kind belonging to a man, another to a woman, and so on with the rest, or is it just the same, too, in the case of health and size and strength? Do you consider that there is one health for a man, and another for a woman? Or, wherever we find health, is it of the same character universally,

greek_head: ᾖ ἐάντε ἐν ἀνδρὶ ἐάντε ἐν ἄλλῳ ὁτῳοῦν ᾖ Ἡ αὐτή μοι δοκεῖ ὑγίειά γε εἶναι καὶ ἀνδρὸς καὶ
greek_tokens: eimi ean en andris ean en allos ostis eimi o autos egw dokew ugieia ge eimi kai anhr kai gunh oukoun kai megeqos kai isxus ean isxuros gunh eimi o autos eidomai kai o autos isxus isxuros eimi o gar o autos outos legos oudeis diaferw pros o isxus eimi o isxus ean en andris eimi ean en gunh h dokew tis su diaferw ou egw
english_head: in a man or in anyone else? I think that health is the same, both in man and in woman. Then is it not so with size and strength also? If a woman is strong, she will be strong by reason of the same form and the same strength; by the same I mean that strength does not differ as strength, whether it be in a man or in a woman. Or do you think there is any difference? I do not.

greek_head: Ἡ δὲ ἀρετὴ πρὸς τὸ ἀρετὴ εἶναι διοίσει τι ἐάντε ἐν παιδὶ ᾖ ἐάντε ἐν πρεσβύτῃ ἐάντε ἐν γυναικὶ ἐάντε ἐν
greek_tokens: o de areth pros o areth eimi diaferw tis ean en pais eimi ean en presbuths ean en gunh ean en andris egw pws dokew eimi swkraths outos ouketi omoios eimi o allos outos tis de ou anhr men areth legw polis eu dioikew gunh de oikia egw ara oun oios te eu dioikew h polis h oikia h allos otioun mh swfrwn kai dikaios dioikew ou dhta
english_head: And will virtue, as virtue, differ at all whether it be in a child or in an elderly person, in a woman or in a man? I feel somehow, Socrates, that here we cease to be on the same ground as in those other cases. Why? Were you not saying that a man’s virtue is to manage a state well, and a woman’s a house? I was. And is it possible to manage a state well, or a house, or anything at all, if you do not manage it temperately and justly?

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἄνπερ δικαίως καὶ σωφρόνως διοικῶσιν δικαιοσύνῃ καὶ σωφροσύνῃ διοικήσουσιν Ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: oukoun ean dikaios kai swfrwn dioikew dikaiosunh kai swfrosunos dioikew anagkh o autos ara amfoteros deomai eiper mellw agaqos eimi kai o gunh kai o anhr dikaiosunh kai swfrosunos fainw tis de pas kai presbuths mhs akolastos eimi kai adikos agaqos an pote gignomai ou dhta alla swfrwn kai
english_head: Surely not. Then whoever manages temperately and justly will manage with temperance and justice? That must be. Then both the woman and the man require the same qualities of justice and temperance, if they are to be good. Evidently. And what of a child or an old man? Can they ever hope to be good if they are intemperate and unjust? Surely not. Only if they are temperate and just? Yes.

greek_head: δίκαιοι Ναί Πάντες ἄρ' ἄνθρωποι τῷ αὐτῷ τρόπῳ ἀγαθοί εἰσιν τῶν αὐτῶν γὰρ τυχόντες ἀγαθοὶ γίγνονται
greek_tokens: dikaios nai pas ara anqrwpos o autos tropos agaqos eimi o autos gar tugxanw agaqos gignomai eoika ou an dhpou ei ge mh o autos areth eimi autos o autos an tropos agaqos eimi ou dhta epei toinun o autos areth pas eimi peiraw eipon kai anamimnhskw tis autos fhmi gorgihs eimi kai su meta ekeinos tis allos ge h arxw oios su eimi o anqrwpos
english_head: So all mankind are good in the same way; for they become good when they acquire the same qualities. So it seems. And I presume, if they had not the same virtue, they would not be good in the same way. No, indeed. Seeing then that it is the same virtue in all cases, try and tell me, if you can recollect, what Gorgias—and you in agreement with him—say it is. Simply that it is the power of governing mankind—

greek_head: εἴπερ ἕν γέ τι ζητεῖς κατὰ πάντων Ἀλλὰ μὴν ζητῶ γε ἀλλ' ἆρα καὶ παιδὸς ἡ αὐτὴ
greek_tokens: eiper eis ge tis zhtew kata pas alla meis zhtew ge alla ara kai pais o autos areth eimi menw kai doulos arxw oios te eimi o despoths kai dokew su eti an doulos eimi o arxw ou panu egw dokew eimi swkraths ou gar eoika eimi aristos eti gar kai ode skopaw arxw fhmi oios su eimi ou prosqew autose o dikaios adikos de mh oimh egw o gar dikaiosunh eimi swkraths areth eimi
english_head: if you want some single description to cover all cases. That is just what I am after. But is virtue the same in a child, Meno, and in a slave—an ability to govern each his master? And do you think he who governed would still be a slave? I should say certainly not, Socrates. No, indeed, it would be unlikely, my excellent friend. And again, consider this further point: you say it is to be able to govern; shall we not add to that—justly, not unjustly? Yes, I think so; for justice, Socrates, is virtue.

greek_head: Πότερον ἀρετή ὦ Μένων ἢ ἀρετή τις Πῶς τοῦτο λέγεις
greek_tokens: poteros areth eimi menw h areth tis pws outos legw ws peri allos ostis oios ei boulomai strogguloths peri eipon an egw ostis sxhma tis eimi ou outws aplos ostis sxhma dia outos de outws an eipon ostis kai allos eimi sxhma orqos ge legos su epei kai egw legos ou monos dikaiosunh alla kai allos eimi areth
english_head: Virtue, Meno, or a virtue? What do you mean by that? What I would in any other case. To take roundness, for instance; I should call it a figure, and not figure pure and simple. And I should name it so because there are other figures as well. You would be quite right—just as I say there are other virtues besides justice.

greek_head: Τίνας ταύτας εἰπέ οἷον καὶ ἐγώ σοι εἴποιμι ἂν καὶ ἄλλα σχήματα εἴ με κελεύοις καὶ σὺ οὖν ἐμοὶ εἰπὲ
greek_tokens: tis outos eipon oios kai egw su eipon an kai allos sxhma ei egw keleuw kai su oun egw eipon allos areth o andreia toinun egw dokew areth eimi kai swfrosunos kai sofia kai megaloprepeia kai allos pampolus palin eimi menw tautos pasxw polus au euriskw areth eis zhtew allos tropos h nundi o de eis o dia pas outos eimi ou dunamai aneuriskw ou gar dunamai pw eimi swkraths ws su zhtew
english_head: What are they? Tell me. In the same way as I can tell you of other figures, if you request me, so do you tell me of other virtues. Well then, courage, I consider, is a virtue, and temperance, and wisdom, and loftiness of mind; and there are a great many others. Once more, Meno, we are in the same plight: again we have found a number of virtues when we were looking for one, though not in the same way as we did just now; but the one that runs through them all, this we are not able to find.

greek_head: μίαν ἀρετὴν λαβεῖν κατὰ πάντων ὥσπερ ἐν τοῖς ἄλλοις Εἰκότως γε ἀλλ' ἐγὼ προθυμήσομαι ἐὰν οἷός τ'
greek_tokens: eis areth lambanw kata pas wsper en o allos eoikotws ge alla egw proqumeomai ean oios su eimi egw probibazw manqanw gar pou ostis outwsi exis peri pas ei tis su aneromai outos o nundi egw elegos tis eimi sxhma eimi menw ei autos eipon ostis strogguloths ei su eipon o egw poteros sxhma o strogguloths eimi h sxhma tis eipon dhpou an ostis sxhma tis panu ge
english_head: No, for I am not yet able, Socrates, to follow your line of search, and find a single virtue common to all, as one can in other cases. And no wonder; but I will make an effort, so far as I can, to help us onward. You understand, of course, that this principle of mine applies to everything: if someone asked you the question I put to you just now: What is figure, Meno? and you replied: Roundness; and then he said, as I did: Is roundness figure or a figure? I suppose you would answer: A figure. Certainly.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν διὰ ταῦτα ὅτι καὶ ἄλλα ἔστιν σχήματα Ναί
greek_tokens: oukoun dia outos ostis kai allos eimi sxhma nai kai ei ge prosanerwtaw su opoios legw an egw kai au ei peri xrwma wsautws anairw ostis eimi kai eipon su ostis o leukon meta outos upolambanw o erwtaw poteros o leukon xrwma eimi h xrwma tis eipon an ostis xrwma tis dioti kai allos tugxanw eimi egw kai ei ge su keleuw legw allos xrwma legw
english_head: And for this reason—that there are other figures as well? Yes. And if he went on to ask you of what sort they were, you would tell him? I would. And if he asked likewise what color is, and on your answering white your questioner then rejoined: Is white color or a color? your reply would be: A color; because there are other colors besides. It would. And if he bade you mention other colors,

greek_head: ἂν ἄλλα ἃ οὐδὲν ἧττον τυγχάνει ὄντα χρώματα τοῦ λευκοῦ Ναί
greek_tokens: an allos o oudeis hsswn tugxanw eimi xrwma o leukon nai ei oun wsper egw meteimi o logos kai legw ostis aei eis polus afikneomai alla mh egw outws alla epei o polus outos eis tis prosagoreuw onoma kai fhmi oudeis autos ostis ou sxhma eimi kai outos kai enantios eimi allhlwn ostis eimi outos o oudeis hsswn kataxew o stroggulos h o euqus o dh onomazw sxhma
english_head: you would tell him of others that are colors just as much as white? Yes. Now suppose that, like me, he pursued the argument and said: We are always arriving at a variety of things, but let me have no more of that: since you call these many things by one single name, and say they are figures, every one of them, even when they are opposed to one another, tell me what is that which comprises round and straight alike, and which you call figure—

greek_head: καὶ οὐδὲν μᾶλλον φῂς τὸ στρογγύλον σχῆμα εἶναι ἢ τὸ εὐθύ ἢ οὐχ οὕτω λέγεις
greek_tokens: kai oudeis mallon fhmi o stroggulos sxhma eimi h o euqus h ou outws legw egw ara oun otan outws legos tote oudeis mallon fhmi o stroggulos eimi stroggulos h euqus oude o euqus euqus h stroggulos ou dhpou eimi swkraths alla meis sxhma ge oudeis mallon fhmi eimi o stroggulos o euqhs oude o eteros o eteros alhqhs legw tis pote oun outos e outos onoma eimi o sxhma
english_head: including straight equally with round under that term. For that is your statement, is it not? It is. And in making it, do you mean to say that round is no more round than straight, or straight no more straight than round? No, to be sure, Socrates. What you mean is that the round shape is no more a figure than the straight, or the straight than the round. Quite right. Then what can this thing be, which bears the name of figure? Try and tell me. Suppose that, on being asked this question by someone,

greek_head: πειρῶ λέγειν εἰ οὖν τῷ ἐρωτῶντι οὕτως ἢ περὶ σχήματος ἢ χρώματος εἶπες ὅτι Ἀλλ' οὐδὲ μανθάνω ἔγωγε ὅτι
greek_tokens: peiraw legw ei oun o erwtaw outws h peri sxhma h xrwma eipon ostis alla oude manqanw egw ostis boulomai eimi anqrwpos oude oida ostis legw isos an qaumazw kai eipon ou manqanw ostis zhtew o epi pas outos tautos h oude epi outos eimi menw exw an eipon ei tis su erwtaw tis eimi epi o stroggulos kai euqhs kai epi o allos o dh sxhma kalew tautos epi pas peiraw eipon ina kai gignomai su meleth pros o peri o aretaw apokrisls
english_head: either about figure or about color, you had replied: Why, I don’t so much as understand what you want, sir, or even know what you are saying: he might well have shown surprise, and said: Do you not understand that I am looking for that which is the same common element in all these things? Or would you still be unable to reply, Meno, if you were approached on other terms, and were asked: What is it that is common to the round and the straight and everything else that you call figures—the same in all? Try and tell me it will be good practice for your answer about virtue.

greek_head: Μή ἀλλὰ σύ ὦ Σώκρατες εἰπέ Βούλει σοι χαρίσωμαι

english_head: No, it is you who must answer, Socrates. You wish me to do you the favour? By all means. And then you will agree to take your turn and answer me on virtue? I will. Well then, I must make the effort, for it is worth our while. Certainly. Come now, let me try and tell you what figure is. Just consider if you accept this description of it: figure, let us say, is the only existing thing that is found always following color. Are you satisfied, or are you looking for something different? I am sure I should be content with a similar account of virtue from you.

greek_head: οὕτως ἀγαπῴην εἴ μοι ἀρετὴν εἴποις Ἀλλὰ τοῦτό γε εὔηθες ὦ Σώκρατες

english_head: But it is such a silly one, Socrates. How do you mean? Well, figure, as I understand by your account, is what always follows color. Very good; but if some one said he did not know color, and was in the same difficulty about it as about figure, what answer do you suppose would have come from you? The truth, from me; and if my questioner were a professor of the eristic and contentious sort, I should say to him:

greek_head: αὐτῷ ὅτι Ἐμοὶ μὲν εἴρηται εἰ δὲ μὴ ὀρθῶς λέγω σὸν ἔργον λαμβάνειν λόγον καὶ ἐλέγχειν εἰ δὲ ὥσπερ ἐγώ

english_head: I have made my statement; if it is wrong, your business is to examine and refute it. But if, like you and me on this occasion, we were friends and chose to have a discussion together, I should have to reply in some milder tone more suited to dialectic. The more dialectical way, I suppose, is not merely to answer what is true, but also to make use of those points which the questioned person acknowledges he knows. And this is the way in which I shall now try to argue with you. Tell me, is there something you call an end? Such a thing, I mean,

greek_head: λέγε γάρ μοι τελευτὴν καλεῖς τι τοιόνδε λέγω οἷον πέρας καὶ ἔσχατον πάντα ταῦτα ταὐτόν τι λέγω ἴσως δ' ἂν

english_head: as a limit, or extremity—I use all these terms in the same sense, though I daresay Prodicus might quarrel with us. But you, I am sure, refer to a thing as terminated or ended: something of that sort is what I mean—nothing complicated. Yes, I do, and I think I grasp your meaning.

english_head: Well then, you speak of a surface, and also of a solid—the terms employed in geometrical problems? I do. So now you are able to comprehend from all this what I mean by figure. In every instance of figure I call that figure in which the solid ends; and I may put that more succinctly by saying that figure is limit of solid. And what do you say of color, Socrates? How overbearing of you, Meno, to press an old man with demands for answers, when you will not trouble yourself

One might tell even blindfolded, Meno, by the way you discuss, that you are handsome and still have lovers. Why so? Because you invariably speak in a peremptory tone, after the fashion of spoilt beauties, holding as they do a despotic power so long as their bloom is on them. You have also, I daresay,

Do not both of you say there are certain effluences of existent things, as Empedocles held? Certainly. And passages into which and through which the effluences pass? To be sure. And some of the effluences fit into various passages,

english_head: while some are too small or too large? That is so. And further, there is what you call sight? Yes. So now conceive my meaning, as Pindar says: color is an effluence of figures, commensurate with sight and sensible. Your answer, Socrates, seems to me excellently put. Yes, for I expect you find its terms familiar; and at the same time I fancy you observe that it enables you to tell what sound and smell are, and numerous other

english_head: things of the kind. Certainly. It is an answer in the high poetic style, Meno, and so more agreeable to you than that about figure. Yes, it is. But yet, son of Alexidemus, I am inclined to think the other was the better of the two; and I believe you also would prefer it, if you were not compelled, as you were saying yesterday, to go away before the mysteries, and could stay awhile and be initiated.

english_head: But I should stay, Socrates, if you would give me many such answers. Well then, I will spare no endeavor, both for your sake and for my own, to continue in that style; but I fear I may not succeed in keeping for long on that level. But come now, you in your turn must try and fulfil your promise by telling me what virtue is in a general way; and you must stop producing a plural from the singular, as the wags say whenever one breaks something, but leave virtue

greek_head: παρ' ἐμοῦ εἴληφας Δοκεῖ τοίνυν μοι ὦ Σώκρατες ἀρετὴ εἶναι καθάπερ
greek_tokens: para egw lambanw dokew toinun egw eimi swkraths areth eimi kaqa o poihths legw xairw te kalew kai dunamai kai egw outos legos areth epiqumew o kalh dunatos eimi porizw ara legw o o kalh epiqumew agaqos epiqumhths eimi malista ge ara ws eimi tis e o kakh epiqumew eteros de e o
english_head: whole and sound, and tell me what it is. The pattern you have now got from me. Well, in my view, Socrates, virtue is, in the poet’s words, to rejoice in things honorable and be able for them ; and that, I say, is virtue—to desire what is honorable and be able to procure it. Do you say that he who desires the honorable is desirous of the good? Certainly. Implying that there are some who desire the evil, and others the good? Do not all men,

greek_head: ἀγαθῶν οὐ πάντες ὤριστε δοκοῦσί σοι τῶν ἀγαθῶν ἐπιθυμεῖν Οὐκ ἔμοιγε Ἀλλά τινες τῶν κακῶν

english_head: in your opinion, my dear sir, desire the good? I think not. There are some who desire the evil? Yes. Thinking the evil to be good, do you mean, or actually recognizing it to be evil, and desiring it nevertheless? Both, I believe. Do you really believe, Meno, that a man knows the evil to be evil, and still desires it? Certainly. What do you mean by desires? Desires the possession of it?

Obviously those who are ignorant of the evil do not desire it, but only what they supposed

greek_tokens: e agnoew autos alla ekeinos o oiomai agaqos eimi eimi de outos ge kakos wste e agnoew autos kai oiomai agaqos eimi dhlos ostis o agaqos epiqumew h ou kinduneuw outos ge tis de e o kakh men epiqumew ws fhmi su hgeomai de o kakos blaptw ekeinos os an gignomai gignwskw dhpou ostis blaptw upo autos
english_head: to be good, though it is really evil; so that those who are ignorant of it and think it good are really desiring the good. Is not that so? It would seem to be so in their case. Well now, I presume those who, as you say, desire the evil, and consider that the evil harms him who gets it, know that they will be harmed by it? They needs must.

english_head: But do they not hold that those who are harmed are miserable in proportion to the harm they suffer? That too must be. And are not the miserable ill-starred? I think so. Then is there anyone who wishes to be miserable and ill-starred? I do not suppose there is, Socrates. No one, then, Meno, desires evil, if no one desires to be such an one: for what is being miserable but desiring evil and obtaining it?

greek_head: ἀληθῆ λέγειν ὦ Σώκρατες καὶ οὐδεὶς βούλεσθαι τὰ κακά

english_head: It seems that what you say is true, Socrates, and that nobody desires evil. Well now, you were saying a moment ago that virtue is the desire and ability for good? Yes, I was. One part of the statement—the desire—belongs to our common nature, and in this respect one man is no better than another? Apparently. But it is plain that if one man is not better than another in this, he must be superior in the ability. Certainly. Then virtue, it seems by your account,

greek_head: δύναμις τοῦ πορίζεσθαι τἀγαθά Παντάπασί μοι δοκεῖ ὦ Σώκρατες οὕτως ἔχειν ὡς σὺ νῦν ὑπολαμβάνεις

english_head: is ability to procure goods. I entirely agree, Socrates, with the view which you now take of the matter. Then let us see whether your statement is true in another respect; for very likely you may be right. You say virtue is the ability to procure goods? I do. And do you not mean by goods such things as health and wealth? Yes, and I include the acquisition of gold and silver, and of state honors and offices. Are there any things besides this sort, that you class as goods? No, I refer only to everything of that sort.

greek_head: πάντα λέγω τὰ τοιαῦτα Εἶεν χρυσίον δὲ δὴ καὶ ἀργύριον πορίζεσθαι ἀρετή ἐστιν ὥς φησι Μένων ὁ τοῦ

english_head: Very well: procuring gold and silver is virtue, according to Meno, the ancestral friend of the Great King. Tell me, do you add to such procuring, Meno, that it is to be done justly and piously, or is this indifferent to you, but even though a man procures these things unjustly, do you call them virtue all the same? Surely not, Socrates. Rather, vice. Yes, of course. Then it seems that justice or temperance or holiness or some other part of virtue must accompany the procuring of these things;

greek_head: ὁσιότητα προσεῖναι ἢ ἄλλο τι μόριον ἀρετῆς εἰ δὲ μή οὐκ ἔσται ἀρετή καίπερ ἐκπορίζουσα τἀγαθά Πῶς

So the procuring of this sort of goods will be no more virtue than the want of them; but it seems that whatever comes accompanied by justice will be virtue,

english_head: I feel my soul and my tongue quite benumbed, and I am at a loss what answer to give you. And yet on countless occasions I have made abundant speeches on virtue to various people—and very good speeches they were, so I thought—but now I cannot say one word as to what it is. You are well advised, I consider, in not voyaging or taking a trip away from home; for if you went on like this as a stranger in any other city you would very likely be taken up for a wizard. You are a rogue, Meno, and had almost deceived me. How is that, Socrates?

Now shall we fill up this vacant space in the corner? By all means. So here we must have four equal spaces?

greek_tokens: ode nai tis oun o oloc ode posaplasios ode gignomai tetraplasios dew de ge diplasios egw gignomai h ou mimnhskw panu ge oukoun eimi outos grammh ek gwnia eis gwnia
english_head: BOY. Yes. Well now, how many times larger is this whole space than this other? Four times. But it was to have been only twice, you remember? To be sure. And does this line, drawn from corner to corner,

greek_head: τινὰ τέμνουσα δίχα ἕκαστον τούτων τῶν χωρίων Ναί Οὐκοῦν τέτταρες αὗται γίγνονται γραμμαὶ ἴσαι
greek_tokens: tis temnw dixa ekastos outos o xwrew nai oukoun tessares outos gignomai grammh isos periexw outos o xwrew gignomai gar skopaw dh phlikos tis eimi outos o xwrew ou manqanw ou tessares eimi outos hmisus ekastos ekastos o grammh apotemnw entos h ou nai posos oun thlikoutos en outos eneimi tessares posos de en ode duo o de tessares o duo tis eimi diplasios ode oun
english_head: cut in two each of these spaces? Yes. And have we here four equal lines containing this space? We have. Now consider how large this space is. I do not understand. Has not each of the inside lines cut off half of each of these four spaces? Yes. And how many spaces of that size are there in this part? Four. And how many in this? Two. And four is how many times two? Twice.

greek_head: ποσάπουν γίγνεται Ὀκτώπουν Ἀπὸ ποίας γραμμῆς Ἀπὸ ταύτης Ἀπὸ τῆς ἐκ γωνίας
greek_tokens: posapous gignomai oktwpous apo poa grammh apo outos apo o ek gwnia eis gwnia teinw o tetrapodos nai kalew de ge outos diametron e sofisths wste ei outos diametros onoma apo o diametron an ws su fhmi eimi pais menwn gignomai an o diplasios xwrew panu men oun eimi swkraths tis su dokew eimi menw eimi ostis doca ou eautou outos apokrinw
english_head: And how many feet is this space? Eight feet. From what line do we get this figure? From this. From the line drawn corner-wise across the (our-foot figure? Yes. The professors call it the diagonal: so if the diagonal is its name, then according to you, Meno’s boy, the double space is the square of the diagonal. Yes, certainly it is, Socrates. What do you think, Meno? Was there any opinion that he did not give as an answer of his own thought?

greek_head: Οὔκ ἀλλ' ἑαυτοῦ Καὶ μὴν οὐκ ᾔδει γε ὡς ἔφαμεν ὀλίγον πρότερον
greek_tokens: ou alla eautou kai meis ou oida ge ws fhmi oligos proteros alhqhs legw eneimi de ge autos outos o doca h ou nai o ou oida ara peri os an mh oida eneimi alhqhs doca peri outos os ou oida fainw kai nun men ge autos wsper onar arti anakinew o doca outos ei de autos tis aneromai pollakis o autos outos kai pollaxh oistos ostis teleutaw oudeis hsswn akribhs
english_head: No, they were all his own. But you see, he did not know, as we were saying a while since. That is true. Yet he had in him these opinions, had he not? Yes. So that he who does not know about any matters, whatever they be, may have true opinions on such matters, about which he knows nothing? Apparently. And at this moment those opinions have just been stirred up in him, like a dream; but if he were repeatedly asked these same questions in a variety of forms, you know he will have in the end as exact an understanding

greek_head: ἐπιστήσεται περὶ τούτων Ἔοικεν
greek_tokens: efisthmi peri outos eoika oukoun oudeis didaskw alla erwtaw efisthmi analambanw autos ek eautou o episthmh nai o de analambanw autos en eautou episthmh ou anamimnhskw eimi panu ge ara oun ou o episthmh os nun outos exis htoi laptw pote h aei exw nai oukoun ei men aei exw aei kai eimi episthmos ei de laptw pote ou an en ge o nun bios lambanw eaw h
english_head: of them as anyone. So it seems. Without anyone having taught him, and only through questions put to him, he will understand, recovering the knowledge out of himself? Yes. And is not this recovery of knowledge, in himself and by himself, recollection? Certainly. And must he not have either once acquired or always had the knowledge he now has? Yes. Now if he always had it, he was always in a state of knowing; and if he acquired it all some time, he could not have acquired it in this life.

greek_head: δεδίδαχέν τις τοῦτον γεωμετρεῖν οὗτος γὰρ ποιήσει περὶ πάσης γεωμετρίας ταὐτὰ ταῦτα καὶ τῶν ἄλλων μαθημάτων
greek_tokens: didaskw tis outos gewmetrew outos gar poihsis peri pas gewmetria tautos outos kai o allos maqhma apas eimi oun ostis outos pas didaskw dikaios gar pou ei oida allos te epei en o sew oikia gignomai kai trefw alla oida egw ostis oudeis pwpote didaskw exis de outos o doca h ou anagkh eimi swkraths fainw ei de mh en o nun bios lambanw ou hdh outos
english_head: Or has someone taught him geometry? You see, he can do the same as this with all geometry and every branch of knowledge. Now, can anyone have taught him all this? You ought surely to know, especially as he was born and bred in your house. Well, I know that no one has ever taught him. And has he these opinions, or has he not? He must have them, Socrates, evidently. And if he did not acquire them in this present life, is it not obvious at once

greek_head: δῆλον ὅτι ἐν ἄλλῳ τινὶ χρόνῳ εἶχε καὶ ἐμεμαθήκει Φαίνεται
greek_tokens: dhlos ostis en allos tis xronos exw kai manqanw fainw oukoun outos ge eimi o xronos oste ou eimi anqrwpos nai ei oun os su an eimi xronos kai os an mh eimi anqrwpos enennumi autos alhqhs doca o erwthsis epegeirw episthmh gignomai ara oun o aei xronos manqanw eimi o yuxh autos dhlos gar ostis o pas xronos eimi h ou eimi anqrwpos fainw
english_head: that he had them and learnt them during some other time? Apparently. And this must have been the time when he was not a human being? Yes. So if in both of these periods—when he was and was not a human being—he has had true opinions in him which have only to be awakened by questioning to become knowledge, his soul must have had this cognizance throughout all time? For clearly he has always either been or not been a human being. Evidently. And if the truth of all things that are is always

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν εἰ ἀεὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια ἡμῖν τῶν ὄντων ἐστὶν ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ ἀθάνατος ἂν ἡ ψυχὴ εἴη ὥστε θαρροῦντα χρὴ ὃ
greek_tokens: oukoun ei aei o alhqeia egw o eimi eimi en o yuxw aqanatos an o yuxh eaw wste qarsew xrh o mh tugxanw efisthmi nun outos de eimi o mh mimnhskw epixeirew zhtew kai anamimnhskw eu egw dokew legw eimi swkraths ou oida opws kai gar egw egw eimi menw kai o men ge allos ou an panu uper o logos diisxurizomai ostis de oiomai dew zhtew o mh tis oida beltiwn an eimi kai andrikos kai hsswn argos h ei oiomai o mh efisthmi mhde
english_head: in our soul, then the soul must be immortal; so that you should take heart and, whatever you do not happen to know at present—that is, what you do not remember—you must endeavor to search out and recollect? What you say commends itself to me, Socrates, I know not how. And so it does to me, Meno. Most of the points I have made in support of my argument are not such as I can confidently assert; but that the belief in the duty of inquiring after what we do not know will make us better and braver and less helpless than the notion that there is not even a possibility of discovering what we do not know,

greek_head: δυνατὸν εἶναι εὑρεῖν μηδὲ δεῖν ζητεῖν περὶ τούτου πάνυ ἂν διαμαχοίμην εἰ οἷός τε εἴην καὶ λόγῳ καὶ ἔργῳ
greek_tokens: dunatos eimi euriskw mhde dew zhtew peri outos panu an diamaxomai ei oios te eimi kai logos kai ergon kai outos men ge dokew egw eu legw eimi swkraths boulomai oun epei omonoew ostis zhthteos peri e mh tis oida epixeirew koinh zhtew tis pote eimi areth panu men oun ou mentoi eimi swkraths alla egw ekeinos an hdus osper arow o proteros kai skeptomai kai akouw poteros ws didaktos eimi autos deomai epixeirew
english_head: nor any duty of inquiring after it—this is a point for which I am determined to do battle, so far as I am able, both in word and deed. There also I consider that you speak aright, Socrates. Then since we are of one mind as to the duty of inquiring into what one does not know, do you agree to our attempting a joint inquiry into the nature of virtue? By all means. But still, Socrates, for my part I would like best of all to examine that question I asked at first, and hear your view as to whether in pursuing it we are to regard it as a thing to be taught, or as a gift of nature to mankind,

greek_head: ἢ ὡς φύσει ἢ ὡς τίνι ποτὲ τρόπῳ παραγιγνομένης τοῖς ἀνθρώποις τῆς ἀρετῆς
greek_tokens: h ws fusis h ws tis pote tropos paragignomai o anqrwpos o aretaw alla ei men egw arxw eimi menw mh monos emautou alla kai sew ou an skeptomai proteros eite didaktos eite ou didaktos o areth prin ostis eimi proteros zhtew autos epei de su sautou men oude epixeirew arxw ina dh eleuqeros eimi egw de epixeirew te arxw kai arxw sugxwrew su tis gar xrh poiew eoika oun skepteos
english_head: or as arriving to them in some other way which I should be glad to know. Had I control over you, Meno, as over myself, we should not have begun considering whether virtue can or cannot be taught until we had first inquired into the main question of what it is. But as you do not so much as attempt to control yourself—you are so fond of your liberty— and both attempt and hold control over me, I will yield to your request—what else am I to do?

greek_head: εἶναι ποῖόν τί ἐστιν ὃ μήπω ἴσμεν ὅτι ἐστίν εἰ μή τι οὖν ἀλλὰ σμικρόν γέ μοι τῆς ἀρχῆς χάλασον καὶ συγχώρησον
greek_tokens: eimi poios tis eimi o mhpw oida ostis eimi ei mh tis oun alla mikros ge egw o arxh xalaw kai sugxwrew ek upoqesis autos skopaw eite didaktos eimi eite opwsoun legos de o ek upoqesis wde wsper e gewmetrhs pollakis skopaw epeidan tis eromai autos oios peri xwrion ei oios te eis ode o kuklos ode o xwrew
english_head: So it seems we are to consider what sort of thing it is of which we do not yet know what it is! Well, the least you can do is to relax just a little of your authority, and allow the question—whether virtue comes by teaching or some other way—to be examined by means of hypothesis. I mean by hypothesis what the geometricians often do in dealing with a question put to them; for example,

greek_head: τρίγωνον ἐνταθῆναι εἴποι ἄν τις ὅτι Οὔπω οἶδα εἰ ἔστιν τοῦτο τοιοῦτον ἀλλ' ὥσπερ μέν τινα ὑπόθεσιν προὔργου
greek_tokens: trigwnos enteinw eipon an tis ostis oupw oida ei eimi outos toioutos alla wsper men tis upoqesis prourgou oimh exw pros o pragma toiosde ei men eimi outos o xwrew toioutos oios para o didwmi autos grammh parateinw elleipw toioutos xwrion oios an autos o parateinw eimi allos tis sumbainw egw dokew kai allos au ei adunatos eimi outos pasxw upotiqhmi oun eqelw
english_head: whether a certain area is capable of being inscribed as a triangular space in a given circle: they reply—I cannot yet tell whether it has that capability; but I think, if I may put it so, that I have a certain helpful hypothesis for the problem, and it is as follows: If this area is such that when you apply it to the given line of the circle you find it falls short by a space similar to that which you have just applied, then I take it you have one consequence, and if it is impossible for it to fall so, then some other. Accordingly I wish to put a hypothesis, before I state our conclusion as regards inscribing this figure

greek_head: εἰπεῖν σοι τὸ συμβαῖνον περὶ τῆς ἐντάσεως αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸν κύκλον εἴτε ἀδύνατον εἴτε μή οὕτω δὴ καὶ περὶ ἀρετῆς
greek_tokens: eipon su o sumbainw peri o entasis autos eis o kuklos eite adunatos eite mh outws dh kai peri aretaw egw epei ou oida oute ostis eimi oute opoios tis upotiqhmi autos skopaw eite didaktos eite ou didaktos eimi wde legw ei poios tis eimi o peri o yuxh eimi areth didaktos an eaw h ou didaktos proteros men dh ei eimi alloios h oios episthmh ara didaktos h ou h o nundi legw anamnhstos diaferw de mhdeis egw
english_head: in the circle by saying whether it is impossible or not. In the same way with regard to our question about virtue, since we do not know either what it is or what kind of thing it may be, we had best make use of a hypothesis in considering whether it can be taught or not, as thus: what kind of thing must virtue be in the class of mental properties, so as to be teachable or not? In the first place, if it is something dissimilar or similar to knowledge, is it taught or not—or, as we were saying just now, remembered? Let us have no disputing about the choice of a name:

greek_head: ὁποτέρῳ ἂν τῷ ὀνόματι χρώμεθα ἀλλ' ἆρα διδακτόν ἢ τοῦτό γε παντὶ δῆλον ὅτι οὐδὲν ἄλλο διδάσκεται ἄνθρωπος
greek_tokens: opoteros an o onoma xraomai alla ara didaktos h outos ge pas dhlos ostis oudeis allos didaskw anqrwpos h episthmh egw dokew ei de ge eimi episthmh tis o areth dhlos ostis didaktos an eaw pws gar ou outos men ara taxus apallassw ostis toiosde men eimi didaktos toiosde de ou panu ge o dh meta outos ws eoika deomai skeptomai poteros eimi episthmh o areth h alloios episthmh
english_head: is it taught? Or is not this fact plain to everyone—that the one and only thing taught to men is knowledge? I agree to that. Then if virtue is a kind of knowledge, clearly it must be taught? Certainly. So you see we have made short work of this question—if virtue belongs to one class of things it is teachable, and if to another, it is not. To be sure. The next question, it would seem, that we have to consider is whether virtue is knowledge, or of another kind than knowledge.

greek_head: Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ τοῦτο μετὰ τοῦτο σκεπτέον εἶναι Τί δὲ δή ἄλλο τι ἢ ἀγαθὸν αὐτό φαμεν εἶναι τὴν
greek_tokens: egw dokew outos meta outos skepteos eimi tis de dh allos tis h agaqos autos fhmi eimi o areth kai outos o upoqesis menos egw agaqos autos eimi panu men oun oukoun ei men tis eimi agaqos kai allos xwrizw episthmh taxa an eaw o areth ou episthmh tis ei de mhdeis eimi agaqos o ou episthmh periexw episthmh an tis autos upopteuw eimi orqos upopteuw eimi outos kai meis
english_head: I should say that is the next thing we have to consider. Well now, surely we call virtue a good thing, do we not, and our hypothesis stands, that it is good? Certainly we do. Then if there is some good apart and separable from knowledge, it may be that virtue is not a kind of knowledge; but if there is nothing good that is not embraced by knowledge, our suspicion that virtue is a kind of knowledge would be well founded. Quite so. Now it is by virtue that we are good? Yes.

greek_head: ἀρετῇ γ' ἐσμὲν ἀγαθοί Ναί Εἰ δὲ ἀγαθοί ὠφέλιμοι πάντα γὰρ τἀγαθὰ ὠφέλιμα οὐχί Ναί
greek_tokens: aretaw ge eimi agaqos nai ei de agaqos wfelimos pas gar agaqos wfelimos ou nai kai o areth dh wfelimos eimi anagkh ek o omologew skeptomai dh kaqa ekastos analambanw poios eimi o egw wfelew ugieia fhmi kai isxus kai kallos kai ploutos dh outos legw kai o toioutos wfelimos
english_head: And if good, profitable; for all good things are profitable, are they not? Yes. So virtue is profitable? That must follow from what has been admitted. Then let us see, in particular instances, what sort of things they are that profit us. Health, let us say, and strength, and beauty, and wealth—these and their like we call profitable, do we not? Yes.

greek_head: οὐχί Ναί Ταὐτὰ δὲ ταῦτά φαμεν ἐνίοτε καὶ βλάπτειν ἢ σὺ ἄλλως φῂς ἢ οὕτως Οὐκ ἀλλ'
greek_tokens: ou nai tautos de outos fhmi eniote kai blaptw h su allos fhmi h outws ou alla outws skopaw dh otan tis ekastos outos hgeomai wfelew egw kai otan tis blaptw ara ou otan men orqos xrhsis wfelew otan de mh blaptw panu ge eti toinun kai o kata o yuxh skeptomai swfrosunos tis kalew kai dikaiosunh kai andreia kai eumaqeia kai mnhmh kai megaloprepeia kai pas o
english_head: But these same things, we admit, actually harm us at times; or do you dispute that statement? No, I agree. Consider now, what is the guiding condition in each case that makes them at one time profitable, and at another harmful. Are they not profitable when the use of them is right, and harmful when it is not? To be sure. Then let us consider next the goods of the soul: by these you understand temperance, justice, courage, intelligence, memory, magnanimity, and so forth?

greek_head: τοιαῦτα Ἔγωγε Σκόπει δή τούτων ἅττα σοι δοκεῖ μὴ ἐπιστήμη εἶναι ἀλλ' ἄλλο ἐπιστήμης εἰ οὐχὶ
greek_tokens: toioutos egw skopaw dh outos ostis su dokew mh episthmh eimi alla allos episthmh ei ou tote men blaptw tote de wfelew oios andreia ei mh eimi fronhsis o andreia alla oios qarsos tis ou otan men aneu neomai qarsew anqrwpos blaptw otan de sun egw wfelew nai oukoun kai swfrosunos wsautws kai eumaqeia meta men neomai kai manqanw kai katartuw wfelimos aneu de neomai blaberos panu
english_head: Yes. Now tell me; such of these as you think are not knowledge, but different from knowledge—do they not sometimes harm us, and sometimes profit us? For example, courage, if it is courage apart from prudence, and only a sort of boldness: when a man is bold without sense, he is harmed; but when he has sense at the same time, he is profited, is he not? Yes. And the same holds of temperance and intelligence: things learnt and coordinated with the aid of sense are profitable, but without sense they are harmful?

greek_head: σφόδρα Οὐκοῦν συλλήβδην πάντα τὰ τῆς ψυχῆς ἐπιχειρήματα καὶ καρτερήματα ἡγουμένης μὲν φρονήσεως εἰς
greek_tokens: sfodra oukoun sullhbdhn pas o o yuxazw epixeirhma kai karterhma hgeomai men fronhsis eis eudaimonia teleutaw afrosunh de eis enantion eoika ei ara areth o en o yuxw tis eimi kai anagkaion autos wfelimos eimi fronhsis autos deomai eimi epei pas o kata o yuxh autos men kaqa eautou oute wfelimos oute blaberos eimi prosgignomai de fronhsis
english_head: Most certainly. And in brief, all the undertakings and endurances of the soul, when guided by wisdom, end in happiness, but when folly guides, in the opposite? So it seems. Then if virtue is something that is in the soul, and must needs be profitable, it ought to be wisdom, seeing that all the properties of the soul are in themselves neither profitable nor harmful, but are made either one or the other

greek_head: ἢ ἀφροσύνης βλαβερά τε καὶ ὠφέλιμα γίγνεται κατὰ δὴ τοῦτον τὸν λόγον ὠφέλιμόν γε οὖσαν τὴν ἀρετὴν
greek_tokens: h afrosunh blaberos te kai wfelimos gignomai kata dh outos o logos wfelimos ge eimi o areth fronhsis deomai tis eimi egw dokew kai men dh kai allos o nundi legw ploutos te kai o toioutos tote men agaqos tote de blaberos eimi ara ou wsper o allh yuxw o fronhsis hgeomai wfelimos o o yuxazw poiew o de afrosunh blaberos outws au
english_head: by the addition of wisdom or folly; and hence, by this argument, virtue being profitable must be a sort of wisdom. I agree. Then as to the other things, wealth and the like, that we mentioned just now as being sometimes good and sometimes harmful—are not these also made profitable or harmful by the soul according as she uses and guides them rightly or wrongly:

greek_head: καὶ τούτοις ἡ ψυχὴ ὀρθῶς μὲν χρωμένη καὶ ἡγουμένη ὠφέλιμα αὐτὰ ποιεῖ μὴ ὀρθῶς δὲ βλαβερά Πάνυ γε
greek_tokens: kai outos o yuxh orqos men xraomai kai hgeomai wfelimos autos poiew mh orqos de blaberos panu ge orqos de ge o emfrwn hgeomai amartanw de o afrwn eimi outos oukoun outws dh kata pas eipon eimi o anqrwpos o men allos pas eis o yuxh anartaw o de o yuxazw autos eis fronhsis ei
english_head: just as, in the case of the soul generally, we found that the guidance of wisdom makes profitable the properties of the soul, while that of folly makes them harmful? Certainly. And the wise soul guides rightly, and the foolish erroneously? That is so. Then may we assert this as a universal rule, that in man all other things depend upon the soul, while the things of the soul herself depend upon wisdom,

greek_head: μέλλει ἀγαθὰ εἶναι καὶ τούτῳ τῷ λόγῳ φρόνησις ἂν εἴη τὸ ὠφέλιμον φαμὲν δὲ τὴν ἀρετὴν ὠφέλιμον εἶναι
greek_tokens: mellw agaqos eimi kai outos o logos fronhsis an eaw o wfelimos fhmi de o areth wfelimos eimi panu ge fronhsis ara fhmi areth eimi htoi sumpas h meros tis dokew egw kalos legw eimi swkraths o legw oukoun ei outos outws exis ou an eien fusis e agaqos ou egw dokew
english_head: if they are to be good; and so by this account the profitable will be wisdom, and virtue, we say, is profitable? Certainly. Hence we conclude that virtue is either wholly or partly wisdom? It seems to me that your statement, Socrates, is excellent. Then if this is so, good men cannot be good by nature. I think not.

greek_head: Καὶ γὰρ ἄν που καὶ τόδ' ἦν εἰ φύσει οἱ ἀγαθοὶ ἐγίγνοντο ἦσάν που ἂν ἡμῖν οἳ ἐγίγνωσκον τῶν νέων τοὺς
greek_tokens: kai gar an pou kai ode eimi ei fusis e agaqos gignomai eimi pou an egw e gignwskw o neos o agaqos o fusis os egw an paralambanw ekeinos apofainw fulassw an en akropolis katashmainw polus mallon h o xrusion ina mhdeis autos diafqeirw alla epei afikneomai eis o hlikia xrhsimos gignomai o polis eoika ge su eimi swkraths ara oun epei ou fusis e agaqos agaqos gignomai
english_head: No, for then, I presume, we should have had this result: if good men were so by nature, we surely should have had men able to discern who of the young were good by nature, and on their pointing them out we should have taken them over and kept them safe in the citadel, having set our mark on them far rather than on our gold treasure, in order that none might have tampered with them, and that when they came to be of age, they might be useful to their country. Yes, most likely, Socrates. So since it is not by nature that the good become good,

greek_head: ἆρα μαθήσει Δοκεῖ μοι ἤδη ἀναγκαῖον εἶναι καὶ δῆλον ὦ
greek_tokens: ara maqhsis dokew egw hdh anagkaion eimi kai dhlos eimi swkraths kata o upoqesis eiper episthmh eimi areth ostis didaktos eimi isos nh dios alla mh outos ou kalos omologew kai meis dokew ge arti kalos legw alla mh ou en o arti monos deomai autos dokew kalos legw alla kai en o nun kai en o epeita ei mellw tis autos ugihs eimi
english_head: is it by education? We must now conclude, I think, that it is; and plainly, Socrates, on our hypothesis that virtue is knowledge, it must be taught. Yes, I daresay; but what if we were not right in agreeing to that? Well, it seemed to be a correct statement a moment ago. Yes, but not only a moment ago must it seem correct, but now also and hereafter, if it is to be at all sound.

greek_head: Τί οὖν δή πρὸς τί βλέπων δυσχεραίνεις αὐτὸ καὶ ἀπιστεῖς μὴ οὐκ ἐπιστήμη ᾖ ἡ ἀρετή
greek_tokens: tis oun dh pros tis blepw dusxerainw autos kai apistew mh ou episthmh eimi o areth egw su eraomai eimi menw o men gar didaktos autos eimi eiper episthmh eimi ou anatiqhmi mh ou kalos legw ostis de ou eimi episthmh skeptomai ean su dokazw eoikotws apistew ode gar egw eipon ei eimi didaktos otioun pragma mh monos areth ou anagkaion autos kai didaskalos kai maqhths eimi egw dokew
english_head: Why, what reason have you to make a difficulty about it, and feel a doubt as to virtue being knowledge? I will tell you, Meno. I do not withdraw as incorrect the statement that it is taught, if it is knowledge; but as to its being knowledge, consider if you think I have grounds for misgiving. For tell me now: if anything at all, not merely virtue, is teachable, must there not be teachers and learners of it?

english_head: I think so. Then also conversely, if a thing had neither teachers nor learners, we should be right in surmising that it could not be taught? That is so: but do you think there are no teachers of virtue? I must say I have often inquired whether there were any, but for all my pains I cannot find one. And yet many have shared the search with me, and particularly those persons whom I regard as best qualified for the task. english_head: Now tell me, to whom ought we properly to send him for lessons in this virtue? Or is it clear enough, from our argument just now, that he should go to these men who profess to be teachers of virtue and advertise themselves as the common teachers of the Greeks, and are ready to instruct anyone who chooses in return for fees charged on a fixed scale? To whom are you referring, Socrates? Surely you know as well as anyone; they are the men whom people call sophists.

And can we believe that his father chose to train his own son in those feats, and yet made him no better than his neighbors in his own particular accomplishments—if virtue, as alleged, was to be taught? On my word, I think not. Well, there you have a fine teacher of virtue who, you admit, was one of the best men of past times.

You have been in his company, I know, and you see what he is like. Or take another example—

And, you know as well as I, he taught them to be the foremost horsemen of Athens, and trained them to excel in music and gymnastics and all else that comes under the head of the arts; and with all that, had he no desire to make them good men? He wished to, I imagine, but presumably it is not a thing one can be taught. And that you may not suppose it was only a few of the meanest sort of Athenians who failed in this matter,

greek_head: τὸ πρᾶγμα ἐνθυμήθητι ὅτι Θουκυδίδης αὖ δύο ὑεῖς ἔθρεψεν Μελησίαν καὶ Στέφανον καὶ τούτους ἐπαίδευσεν τά τε ἄλλα
greek_tokens: o pragma enqumeomai ostis qoukudidhs au duo uios trefw melhsihs kai stefanos kai outos paideuw o te allos eu kai palaiw kallista aqhnaia o men gar canqias didwmi o de eudwros outos de pou dokew o tote kallista palaiw h ou mimnhskw egw akoh oukoun dhlos ostis outos ou an pote e men dew
english_head: let me remind you that Thucydides’ also brought up two sons, Melesias and Stephanus, and that besides giving them a good general education he made them the best wrestlers in Athens: one he placed with Xanthias, and the other with Eudorus—masters who, I should think, had the name of being the best exponents of the art. You remember them, do you not? Yes, by hearsay. Well, is it not obvious that this father would never have spent his money on having his children taught all those things,

greek_head: δαπανώμενον διδάσκειν ταῦτα μὲν ἐδίδαξε τοὺς παῖδας τοὺς αὑτοῦ οὗ δὲ οὐδὲν ἔδει ἀναλώσαντα ἀγαθοὺς ἄνδρας ποιῆσαι
greek_tokens: dapanaw didaskw outos men didaskw o pais o eautou e de oudeis dew analiskw agaqos anhr poiew outos de ou didaskw ei didaktos eimi alla gar isos o qoukudidhs faulos eimi kai ou eimi autos pleistos filos aqhnaia kai o summaxos kai oikia megas eimi kai dunamai megas en o polis kai en o allos ellhn wste eiper eimi outos didaktos eceuriskw an ostis mellw autos o uios agaqos poiew h o epixwrios tis h o
english_head: and then have omitted to teach them at no expense the others that would have made them good men, if virtue was to be taught? Will you say that perhaps Thucydides was one of the meaner sort, and had no great number of friends among the Athenians and allies? He, who was of a great house and had much influence in our city and all over Greece, so that if virtue were to be taught he would have found out the man who was likely to make his sons good, whether one of our own people

greek_head: ξένων εἰ αὐτὸς μὴ ἐσχόλαζεν διὰ τὴν τῆς πόλεως ἐπιμέλειαν ἀλλὰ γάρ ὦ ἑταῖρε Ἄνυτε μὴ οὐκ ᾖ διδακτὸν ἀρετή
greek_tokens: cenos ei autos mh sxolazw dia o o polis epimeleia alla gar eimi etairos anuw mh ou eimi didaktos areth eimi swkraths radios egw dokew kakos legw anqrwpos egw men oun an su sumbouleuw ei eqelw egw peiqw eulabeomai ws isos men kai en allh polis raios eimi kakos poiew anqrwpos h eu en ode de kai
english_head: or a foreigner, were he himself too busy owing to the cares of state! Ah no, my dear Anytus, it looks as though virtue were not a teachable thing. Socrates, I consider you are too apt to speak ill of people. I, for one, if you will take my advice, would warn you to be careful: in most cities it is probably easier to do people harm than good, and particularly in this one;

greek_head: πάνυ οἶμαι δὲ σὲ καὶ αὐτὸν εἰδέναι Ὦ Μένων Ἄνυτος μέν μοι δοκεῖ χαλεπαίνειν καὶ
greek_tokens: panu oimh de sos kai autos oida eimi menw anutos men egw dokew xalepainw kai oudeis qaumazw oiomai gar egw proteros men kakhgorew outos o anhr epeita hgeomai kai autos eimi eis outos alla outos men ean pote gignwskw oios eimi o kakos legw pauw xalepainw nun de agnoew su de egw eipon ou kai para su eimi kalos agaqos anhr panu ge
english_head: I think you know that yourself. Meno, I think Anytus is angry, and I am not at all surprised: for he conceives, in the first place, that I am speaking ill of these gentlemen; and in the second place, he considers he is one of them himself. Yet, should the day come when he knows what speaking ill means, his anger will cease; at present he does not know. Now you must answer me: are there not good and honorable men among your people also? Certainly.

greek_head: Τί οὖν ἐθέλουσιν οὗτοι παρέχειν αὑτοὺς διδασκάλους τοῖς νέοις καὶ ὁμολογεῖν διδάσκαλοί τε εἶναι καὶ
greek_tokens: tis oun eqelw outos parexw eautou didaskalos o neos kai omologew didaskalos te eimi kai didaktos areth ou ma o dios eimi swkraths alla tote men an autos aekwn ws didaktos tote de ws ou fhmi oun outos didaskalos eimi outos o pragma os mhde autos outos omologew ou egw dokew eimi swkraths tis de dh e sofisths su outos e monos epaggellw dokew didaskalos eimi aretaw
english_head: Well then, are they willing to put themselves forward as teachers of the young, and avow that they are teachers and that virtue is to be taught? No, no, Socrates, I assure you: sometimes you may hear them refer to it as teachable, but sometimes as not. Then are we to call those persons teachers of this thing, when they do not even agree on that great question? I should say not, Socrates. Well, and what of the sophists? Do you consider these, its only professors,

greek_head: Καὶ Γοργίου μάλιστα ὦ Σώκρατες ταῦτα ἄγαμαι ὅτι οὐκ ἄν ποτε αὐτοῦ τοῦτο ἀκούσαις ὑπισχνουμένου ἀλλὰ
greek_tokens: kai gorgihs malista eimi swkraths outos agamai ostis ou an pote autos outos aekwn upisxneomai alla kai o allos katagelaw otan akousis upisxneomai alla legw oiomai dew poiew deinos oude ara sos dokew e sofisths didaskalos eimi ou exw legw eimi swkraths kai gar autos osper e polus pasxw tote men egw dokew tote de ou oida de ostis ou monos sos te kai o allos o politikos outos dokew tote men eimi didaktos tote de ou
english_head: to be teachers of virtue? That is a point, Socrates, for which I admire Gorgias: you will never hear him promising this, and he ridicules the others when he hears them promise it. Skill in speaking is what he takes it to be their business to produce. Then you do not think the sophists are teachers of virtue? I cannot say, Socrates. I am in the same plight as the rest of the world: sometimes I think that they are, sometimes that they are not. And are you aware that not only you and other political folk

greek_head: ἀλλὰ καὶ Θέογνιν τὸν ποιητὴν οἶσθ' ὅτι ταὐτὰ ταῦτα λέγει Ἐν ποίοις ἔπεσιν
greek_tokens: alla kai qeognis o poihths oistos ostis tautos outos legw en poios epos en o elegeion e legw kai para o pinw kai esqiw kai meta o izw kai andanw o os megas dunamis esqlos men gar apais esqlos didaskw ean de kakos
english_head: are in two minds as to whether virtue is to be taught, but Theognis the poet also says, you remember, the very same thing? In which part of his poems? In those elegiac lines where he says—Eat and drink with these men; sit with them, and be pleasing unto them, who wield great power; for from the good wilt thou win thee lessons in the good; but mingle with the bad,

greek_head: συμμίσγῃς ἀπολεῖς καὶ τὸν ἐόντα νόον οἶσθ' ὅτι ἐν τούτοις μὲν ὡς διδακτοῦ οὔσης τῆς ἀρετῆς λέγει
greek_tokens: summignumi apollumi kai o eimi noos oistos ostis en outos men ws didaktos eimi o aretaw legw fainw ge en allos de ge oligos metabainw ei de eimi poihtos fhmi kai enqetos andris nohma legw pws ostis polus an misqos kai megas ferw e dunamai outos poiew kai ou pote an ek agaqos pathr gignomai kakos
english_head: and thou wilt lose even the sense that thou hast.Theognis 33-36 BergkDo you observe how in these words he implies that virtue is to be taught? He does, evidently. But in some other lines he shifts his ground a little, saying—Could understanding be created and put into a manTheognis 434-438 Bergk (I think it runs thus) many high rewards would they obtain

greek_head: πειθόμενος μύθοισι σαόφροσιν ἀλλὰ διδάσκων οὔ ποτε ποιήσεις τὸν κακὸν ἄνδρ' ἀγαθόν
greek_tokens: peiqw muqos swfrwn alla didaskw ou pote poihsis o kakos anhr agaqos ennoew ostis autos eautou palin peri o autos enantios legw fainw exis oun eipon allos ostis pragma e e men faskw didaskalos eimi ou opws allos didaskalos omologew alla oude autos efisthmi alla ponhros
english_head: for he would have followed the precepts of wisdom: but not by teaching wilt thou ever make the had man goodBergk 434-438. You notice how in the second passage he contradicts himself on the same point? Apparently. Well, can you name any other subject in which the professing teachers are not only refused recognition as teachers of others, but regarded as not even understanding it themselves,

greek_head: εἶναι περὶ αὐτὸ τοῦτο τὸ πρᾶγμα οὗ φασι διδάσκαλοι εἶναι οἱ δὲ ὁμολογούμενοι αὐτοὶ καλοὶ κἀγαθοὶ τοτὲ μέν φασιν
greek_tokens: eimi peri autos outos o pragma e fhmi didaskalos eimi e de omologew autos kalos agaqos tote men fhmi autos didaktos eimi tote de ou o oun outws tarassw peri ostis fhmi an su kurios didaskalos eimi ma diw ou egw oukoun ei mhte e sofisths mhte e autos kalos agaqos eimi didaskalos eimi o pragma dhlos ostis ou an allos ge ou egw dokew
english_head: and indeed as inferior in the very quality of which they claim to be teachers; while those who are themselves recognized as men of worth and honor say at one time that it is teachable, and at another that it is not? When people are so confused about this or that matter, can you say they are teachers in any proper sense of the word? No, indeed, I cannot. Well, if neither the sophists nor the men who are themselves good and honorable are teachers of the subject, clearly no others can be? I agree.

greek_head: Εἰ δέ γε μὴ διδάσκαλοι οὐδὲ μαθηταί Δοκεῖ μοι ἔχειν ὡς λέγεις
greek_tokens: ei de ge mh didaskalos oude maqhths dokew egw exw ws legw omologew de ge pragma e mhte didaskalos mhte maqhths eien outos mhde didaktos eimi omologew oukoun aretaw oudamos fainw didaskalos eimi outos ei de ge mh didaskalos oude maqhths fainw outws areth ara ou an eaw didaktos
english_head: And if there are no teachers, there can be no disciples either? I think that statement is true. And we have admitted that a thing of which there are neither teachers nor disciples cannot be taught? We have. So nowhere are any teachers of virtue to be found? That is so. And if no teachers, then no disciples? So it appears. Hence virtue cannot be taught?

greek_head: Οὐκ ἔοικεν εἴπερ ὀρθῶς ἡμεῖς ἐσκέμμεθα ὥστε καὶ θαυμάζω δή ὦ Σώκρατες πότερόν ποτε οὐδ' εἰσὶν ἀγαθοὶ
greek_tokens: ou eoika eiper orqos egw skeptomai wste kai qaumazw dh eimi swkraths poteros pote oude eimi agaqos anhr h tis an eaw tropos o genesis o agaqos gignomai kinduneuw eimi menw egw te kai su faulos tis eimi anhr kai sos te gorgihs ou ikanos paideuw kai egw prodikos pas mallon oun prosekteos o new egw autos kai zhthteos ostis egw eis ge tis tropos beltiwn
english_head: It seems likely, if our investigation is correct. And that makes me wonder, I must say, Socrates, whether perhaps there are no good men at all, or by what possible sort of process good people can come to exist? I fear, Meno, you and I are but poor creatures, and Gorgias has been as faulty an educator of you as Prodicus of me. So our first duty is to look to ourselves, and try to find somebody who will have some means or other of making us better.

greek_head: ποιήσει λέγω δὲ ταῦτα ἀποβλέψας πρὸς τὴν ἄρτι ζήτησιν ὡς ἡμᾶς ἔλαθεν καταγελάστως ὅτι οὐ μόνον ἐπιστήμης
greek_tokens: poihsis legos de outos apoblepw pros o arti zhthsis ws egw elaunw katagelastos ostis ou monos episthmh hgeomai orqos te kai eu o anqrwpos prassw o pragma h isos kai diafeugw egw o gignwskw tis pote tropos gignomai e agaqos anhr pws outos legw eimi swkraths wde ostis men o agaqos anhr deomai wfelimos eimi
english_head: I say this with special reference to our recent inquiry, in which I see that we absurdly failed to note that it is not only through the guidance of knowledge that human conduct is right and good; and it is probably owing to this that we fail to perceive by what means good men can be produced. To what are you alluding, Socrates? I mean that good men must be useful: we were right, were we not, in admitting that

greek_head: ὀρθῶς ὡμολογήκαμεν τοῦτό γε ὅτι οὐκ ἂν ἄλλως ἔχοι ἦ γάρ Ναί
greek_tokens: orqos omologew outos ge ostis ou an allos exw eimi gar nai kai ostis ge wfelimos eimi an orqos egw hgeomai o pragma kai outos pou kalos omologew nai ostis de ou eimi orqos hgeomai ean mh fronimos eimi outos omoios eimi ou orqos omologew pws dh orqos legw egw eraomai ei eidoi o odos o eis larisa h opoi boulomai allote badizw kai allos hgeomai allos tis orqos an kai eu hgeomai panu ge
english_head: this must needs be so? Yes. And in thinking that they will be useful if they give us right guidance in conduct: here also, I suppose, our admission was correct? Yes. But our assertion that it is impossible to give right guidance unless one has knowledge looks very like a mistake. What do you mean by that? I will tell you. If a man knew the way to Larisa, or any other place you please, and walked there and led others, would he not give right and good guidance? Certainly.

greek_head: Τί δ' εἴ τις ὀρθῶς μὲν δοξάζων ἥτις ἐστὶν ἡ ὁδός ἐληλυθὼς δὲ μὴ μηδ' ἐπιστάμενος οὐ καὶ οὗτος ἂν ὀρθῶς
greek_tokens: tis de ei tis orqos men docazw ostis eimi o odos erxomai de mh mhde efisthmi ou kai outos an orqos hgeomai panu ge kai ews ge an pou orqos doca exis peri os o eteros episthmh oudeis xeirwn hgemwn eimi oiomai men alhqhs fronew de mh o outos fronew oudeis gar doca ara alhqhs pros orqoths pracis oudeis xeirwn hgemwn fronhsis kai outos eimi o nundi paraleipw en o peri o aretaw skeptomai opoios tis eaw legw
english_head: Well, and a person who had a right opinion as to which was the way, but had never been there and did not really know, might give right guidance, might he not? Certainly. And so long, I presume, as he has right opinion about that which the other man really knows, he will be just as good a guide—if he thinks the truth instead of knowing it—as the man who has the knowledge. Just as good. Hence true opinion is as good a guide to rightness of action as knowledge; and this is a point we omitted just now in our consideration of the nature of virtue,

greek_head: ὅτι φρόνησις μόνον ἡγεῖται τοῦ ὀρθῶς πράττειν τὸ δὲ ἄρα καὶ δόξα ἦν ἀληθής
greek_tokens: ostis fronhsis monos hgeomai o orqos prassw o de ara kai doca eimi alhqhs eoika ge oudeis ara hsswn wfelimos eimi orqos doca episthmh tosoutos ge eimi swkraths ostis o men o episthmh exw aei an epitugxanw o de o orqos doca tote men an tugxanw tote de ou pws legw o aei exw orqos doca ou aei an tugxanw ewsper orqos docazw anagkh egw fainw wste qaumazw eimi swkraths
english_head: when we stated that knowledge is the only guide of right action; whereas we find there is also true opinion. So it seems. Then right opinion is just as useful as knowledge. With this difference, Socrates, that he who has knowledge will always hit on the right way, whereas he who has right opinion will sometimes do so, but sometimes not. How do you mean? Will not he who always has right opinion be always right, so long as he opines rightly? It appears to me that he must; and therefore I wonder, Socrates,

greek_head: τούτου οὕτως ἔχοντος ὅτι δή ποτε πολὺ τιμιωτέρα ἡ ἐπιστήμη τῆς ὀρθῆς δόξης καὶ δι' ὅτι τὸ μὲν ἕτερον τὸ δὲ
greek_tokens: outos outws exw ostis dh pote polus timios o episthmh o orqos doca kai dia ostis o men eteros o de eteros eimi autos oida oun dia ostis qaumazw h egw su eipon panu ge eipon ostis o daidalos agalma ou prosexw o new isos de oude eimi para su pros tis de dh outos legw ostis kai outos ean men mh dew eimi apodidraskw kai drapeteuw ean de dew paramenw
english_head: this being the case, that knowledge should ever be more prized than right opinion, and why they should be two distinct and separate things. Well, do you know why it is that you wonder, or shall I tell you? Please tell me. It is because you have not observed with attention the images of Daedalus. But perhaps there are none in your country. What is the point of your remark? That if they are not fastened up they play truant and run away; but, if fastened, they stay where they are.

greek_head: Τί οὖν δή Τῶν ἐκείνου ποιημάτων λελυμένον μὲν ἐκτῆσθαι οὐ
greek_tokens: tis oun dh o ekeinos poihma luw men ktaomai ou polus tis acios eimi timaw wsper drapeths anqrwpos ou gar paramenw dew de polus acios panu gar kalos o ergon eimi pros tis oun dh legos outos pros o doca o alhqhs kai gar o doca o alhqhs osos men an xronos paramenw kalos o xrhma kai panth
english_head: Well, what of that? To possess one of his works which is let loose does not count for much in value; it will not stay with you any more than a runaway slave: but when fastened up it is worth a great deal, for his productions are very fine things And to what am I referring in all this? To true opinion. For these, so long as they stay with us, are a fine possession,

greek_head: ἀγαθὰ ἐργάζονται πολὺν δὲ χρόνον οὐκ ἐθέλουσι παραμένειν ἀλλὰ δραπετεύουσιν ἐκ τῆς ψυχῆς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου
greek_tokens: agaqos ergazomai polus de xronos ou eqelw paramenw alla drapeteuw ek o yuxazw o anqrwpos wste ou polus acios eimi ews an tis autos deomai aitios logismos outos de eimi eimi menw etairos anamnhsis ws en o prosqen egw omologew epeidan de dew proteros men episthmh gignomai epeita monimos kai dia outos dh timios episthmh orqos doca eimi kai diaferw desmos episthmh orqos doca nh o dios eimi swkraths eoika toioutos tis
english_head: and effect all that is good; but they do not care to stay for long, and run away out of the human soul, and thus are of no great value until one makes them fast with causal reasoning. And this process, friend Meno, is recollection, as in our previous talk we have agreed. But when once they are fastened, in the first place they turn into knowledge, and in the second, are abiding. And this is why knowledge is more prized than right opinion: the one transcends the other by its trammels. Upon my word, Socrates, it seems to be very much as you say.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν καὶ ἐγὼ ὡς οὐκ εἰδὼς λέγω ἀλλὰ εἰκάζων ὅτι δέ ἐστίν τι ἀλλοῖον ὀρθὴ δόξα καὶ ἐπιστήμη οὐ πάνυ
greek_tokens: kai meis kai egw ws ou eidoi legos alla eikazw ostis de eimi tis alloios orqos doca kai episthmh ou panu egw dokazw outos eikazw alla eiper tis allos fhmi an oida oligos de an fhmi eis de oun kai outos ekeinos qew an os oida kai orqos ge eimi swkraths legw tis de ode ou orqos ostis alhqhs doca hgeomai o ergnumi ekastos o pracis oudeis xeirwn apergazomai h episthmh kai outos dokew egw alhqhs legw
english_head: And indeed I too speak as one who does not know but only conjectures: yet that there is a difference between right opinion and knowledge is not at all a conjecture with me but something I would particularly assert that I knew: there are not many things of which I would say that, but this one, at any rate, I will include among those that I know. Yes, and you are right, Socrates, in so saying. Well, then, am I not right also in saying that true opinion leading the way renders the effect of each action as good as knowledge does? There again, Socrates, I think you speak the truth.

greek_head: Οὐδὲν ἄρα ὀρθὴ δόξα ἐπιστήμης χεῖρον οὐδὲ ἧττον ὠφελίμη ἔσται εἰς τὰς πράξεις οὐδὲ ἁνὴρ ὁ ἔχων ὀρθὴν
greek_tokens: oudeis ara orqos doca episthmh xeirwn oude hsswn wfelimos eimi eis o prassw oude anhr o exw orqos doca h o episthmh eimi outos kai meis o ge agaqos anhr wfelimos egw omologew eimi nai epei toinun ou monos dia episthmh agaqos anhr an eien kai wfelimos o polis eiper eien alla kai dia orqos doca outos de oudeteros fusis eimi o anqrwpos
english_head: So that right opinion will be no whit inferior to knowledge in worth or usefulness as regards our actions, nor will the man who has right opinion be inferior to him who has knowledge. That is so. And you know that the good man has been admitted by us to be useful. Yes. Since then it is not only because of knowledge that men will be good and useful to their country, where such men are to be found, but also on account of right opinion; and since neither of these two things—knowledge

greek_head: οὔτε ἐπιστήμη οὔτε δόξα ἀληθής οὔτ' ἐπίκτητα ἢ δοκεῖ σοι φύσει ὁποτερονοῦν αὐτοῖν εἶναι
greek_tokens: oute episthmh oute doca alhqhs oute epikthtos h dokew su fusis opoteros autos eimi ou egw oukoun epei ou fusis oude e agaqos fusis eien an ou dhta epei de ge ou fusis skopaw o meta outos ei didaktos eimi nai oukoun didaktos dokew eimi ei fronhsis o areth nai kan ei ge didaktos eaw fronhsis an eimi panu ge
english_head: and true opinion—is a natural property of mankind, being acquired—or do you think that either of them is natural? Not I. Then if they are not natural, good people cannot be good by nature either. Of course not. And since they are not an effect of nature, we next considered whether virtue can be taught. Yes. And we thought it teachable if virtue is wisdom? Yes. And if teachable, it must be wisdom? Certainly. And if there were teachers, it could be taught,

greek_head: Καὶ εἰ μέν γε διδάσκαλοι εἶεν διδακτὸν ἂν εἶναι μὴ ὄντων δὲ οὐ διδακτόν
greek_tokens: kai ei men ge didaskalos eien didaktos an eimi mh eimi de ou didaktos outws alla meis omologew mh eimi autos didaskalos eimi outos omologew ara mhte didaktos autos mhte fronhsis eimi panu ge alla meis agaqos ge autos omologew eimi nai wfelimos de kai agaqos eimi o orqos hgeomai panu ge
english_head: but if there were none, it could not? Quite so. But surely we acknowledged that it had no teachers? That is true. Then we acknowledged it neither was taught nor was wisdom? Certainly. But yet we admitted it was a good? Yes. And that which guides rightly is useful and good? Certainly. And that there are only two things—

greek_head: Ὀρθῶς δέ γε ἡγεῖσθαι δύο ὄντα ταῦτα μόνα δόξαν τε ἀληθῆ καὶ ἐπιστήμην ἃ ἔχων ἄνθρωπος ὀρθῶς ἡγεῖται
greek_tokens: orqos de ge hgeomai duo eimi outos monos doca te alhqhs kai episthmh o exw anqrwpos orqos hgeomai o gar apo tuxh tis orqos gignomai ou anqrwpinos hgemonia gignomai os de anqrwpos hgemwn eimi epi o orqos duo outos doca alhqhs kai episthmh dokew egw outws oukoun epei ou didaktos eimi oude episthmh dh eti gignomai o areth ou fainw
english_head: true opinion and knowledge—that guide rightly and a man guides rightly if he have these; for things that come about by chance do not occur through human guidance; but where a man is a guide to what is right we find these two things—true opinion and knowledge. I agree. Well now, since virtue is not taught, we no longer take it to be knowledge? Apparently not.

greek_head: Δυοῖν ἄρα ὄντοιν ἀγαθοῖν καὶ ὠφελίμοιν τὸ μὲν ἕτερον ἀπολέλυται καὶ οὐκ ἂν εἴη ἐν πολιτικῇ πράξει

english_head: So of two good and useful things one has been rejected: knowledge cannot be our guide in political conduct. I think not. Therefore it was not by any wisdom, nor because they were wise, that the sort of men we spoke of controlled their states—Themistocles and the rest of them, to whom our friend Anytus was referring a moment ago. For this reason it was that they were unable to make others like unto themselves—because their qualities were not an effect of knowledge. The case is probably as you say, Socrates. And if not by knowledge, as the only alternative it must have been by good opinion.

greek_head: γίγνεται ᾗ οἱ πολιτικοὶ ἄνδρες χρώμενοι τὰς πόλεις ὀρθοῦσιν οὐδὲν διαφερόντως ἔχοντες πρὸς τὸ φρονεῖν ἢ οἱ

english_head: This is the means which statesmen employ for their direction of states, and they have nothing more to do with wisdom than soothsayers and diviners; for these people utter many a true thing when inspired, but have no knowledge of anything they say. I daresay that is so. And may we, Meno, rightly call those men divine who, having no understanding, yet succeed in many a great deed and word? Certainly. Then we shall be right in calling those divine of whom

greek_head: χρησμῳδοὺς καὶ μάντεις καὶ τοὺς ποιητικοὺς ἅπαντας καὶ τοὺς πολιτικοὺς οὐχ ἥκιστα τούτων φαῖμεν ἂν θείους τε εἶναι
greek_tokens: xrhsmwdos kai mantis kai o poihtikos apas kai o politikos ou hkistos outos fhmi an qeios te eimi kai enqousiazw epipnous eimi kai katexw ek o qeaomai otan katorqow legw polus kai megas pragma mhdeis oida os legw panu ge kai o ge gunh dhpou eimi menw o agaqos anhr qeios kalew kai e lakwn otan tis egkwmiazw agaqos anhr qeios anhr fhmi outos
english_head: we spoke just now as soothsayers and prophets and all of the poetic turn; and especially we can say of the statesmen that they are divine and enraptured, as being inspired and possessed of God when they succeed in speaking many great things, while knowing nought of what they say. Certainly. And the women too, I presume, Meno, call good men divine; and the Spartans, when they eulogize a good man, say—He is a divine person.

english_head: And to all appearance, Socrates, they are right; though perhaps our friend Anytus may be annoyed at your statement. For my part, I care not. As for him, Meno, we will converse with him some other time. At the moment, if through all this discussion our queries and statements have been correct, virtue is found to be neither natural nor taught, but is imparted to us by a divine dispensation without understanding in those who receive it,

greek_head: παραγίγνηται εἰ μή τις εἴη τοιοῦτος τῶν πολιτικῶν ἀνδρῶν οἷος καὶ ἄλλον ποιῆσαι πολιτικόν εἰ δὲ εἴη σχεδὸν ἄν τι

english_head: unless there should be somebody among the statesmen capable of making a statesman of another. And if there should be any such, he might fairly be said to be among the living what Homer says Teiresias was among the dead—He alone has comprehension; the rest are flitting shades. In the same way he on earth, in respect of virtue, will be a real substance among shadows.

greek_head: Κάλλιστα δοκεῖς μοι λέγειν ὦ Σώκρατες Ἐκ μὲν τοίνυν τούτου τοῦ λογισμοῦ ὦ Μένων θείᾳ

english_head: I think you put it excellently, Socrates. Then the result of our reasoning, Meno, is found to be that virtue comes to us by a divine dispensation, when it does come. But the certainty of this we shall only know when, before asking in what way virtue comes to mankind, we set about inquiring what virtue is, in and by itself. It is time now for me to go my way, but do you persuade our friend Anytus of that whereof you are now yourself persuaded, so as

greek_head: πρᾳότερος ᾖ ὡς ἐὰν πείσῃς τοῦτον ἔστιν ὅτι καὶ Ἀθηναίους ὀνήσεις
greek_tokens: praos eimi ws ean peiqw outos eimi ostis kai aqhnaios onhsis
english_head: to put him in a gentler mood; for if you can persuade him, you will do a good turn to the people of Athens also.
